items.title: Defining Fever of Unknown Origin (FUO)
items.content: FUO is characterized by a prolonged febrile illness with an uncertain diagnosis despite extensive evaluation. The updated definition includes specific criteria for fever, duration, immune status, and diagnostic testing.
keyPoints: Fever ≥38.3°C (≥101°F) on at least two occasions
Illness duration of at least 3 weeks
No known immunocompromised state

Uncertain diagnosis after thorough history, physical examination, and obligatory investigations including ESR, CRP, platelet count, leukocyte count and differential, hemoglobin, electrolytes, creatinine, total protein, alkaline phosphatase, alanine aminotransferase, aspartate aminotransferase, lactate dehydrogenase, creatine kinase, ferritin, antinuclear antibodies, rheumatoid factor, protein electrophoresis, urinalysis, blood cultures (n=3), urine culture, chest x-ray, abdominal ultrasonography, and TST or IGRA
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · Fever of Unknown Origin | DEFINITION

items.title: Inflammation of Unknown Origin (IUO) vs. FUO
items.content: IUO is similar to FUO but involves elevated inflammatory markers without fever. Both conditions share the same diagnostic workup due to overlapping causes.
keyPoints: IUO defined by elevated CRP or ESR for at least 3 weeks in an immunocompetent patient with normal body temperature
No final explanation despite history, physical examination, and obligatory tests
Causes and workup for IUO are the same as FUO
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · Fever of Unknown Origin | DEFINITION

items.title: Geographic Variation in FUO Etiologies and Prevalence
items.content: The etiology of Fever of Unknown Origin (FUO) varies significantly by geographic region, influenced by local disease epidemiology and healthcare practices. Infections remain the most common cause outside Western nations, with Mycobacterium tuberculosis being a significant contributor in Southern Asia.
keyPoints: Prevalence of FUO is unknown but reported as 2.9% in Japan during the 1990s.
In non-Western cohorts, infections are the most common cause of FUO, with up to half caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
Risk of infection is over four times higher in Southern Asia compared to Europe.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Changing Etiologies and Diagnostic Challenges in FUO
items.content: The range of FUO etiologies has evolved due to improved diagnostic techniques, leading to earlier detection of conditions like intraabdominal abscesses. However, this has resulted in the 'FUO paradox,' where a higher proportion of patients remain undiagnosed despite advanced diagnostics.
keyPoints: Intraabdominal abscesses and tumors are less frequent due to CT and ultrasound.
The 'FUO paradox' describes the increased proportion of undiagnosed cases in Western cohorts, up to >50%.
Noninfectious inflammatory diseases (NIIDs) form the most common cause of FUO in Western cohorts.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Outcomes in FUO
items.content: Despite advanced diagnostic tools, a significant number of FUO cases remain undiagnosed. Recurrent inflammation and symptom duration over 12 months are associated with lower chances of finding a final diagnosis.
keyPoints: Recurrent inflammation reduces the chance of finding a final explanation for FUO.
Symptom duration >12 months is significantly associated with a lower chance of finding a final explanation.
Normal PET/CT scans are linked to a lower likelihood of reaching a final diagnosis.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Common Infectious Causes of Fuo
items.content: Fever of Unknown Origin (FUO) often results from atypical presentations of common diseases. Key infectious causes include endocarditis, diverticulitis, vertebral osteomyelitis, and extrapulmonary tuberculosis.
keyPoints: Endocarditis, diverticulitis, vertebral osteomyelitis, and extrapulmonary tuberculosis are more common infectious diagnoses in Fuo.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Considerations for Culture-Negative Endocarditis
items.content: Culture-negative endocarditis can be challenging due to difficult-to-culture bacteria and non-infectious causes. It may involve HACEK organisms, Coxiella burnetii, T. whipplei, Bartonella species, or paraneoplastic phenomena like marantic endocarditis.
keyPoints: Culture-negative endocarditis may be due to difficult-to-culture bacteria such as nutritionally variant bacteria and HACEK organisms.
Coxiella burnetii, T. whipplei, Bartonella species are potential causes.
Marantic endocarditis is a sterile thrombotic disease associated with adenocarcinomas.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Autoinflammatory Syndromes and VEXAS Syndrome in Fuo
items.content: Adult-onset Still's disease, large-vessel vasculitis, polymyalgia rheumatica, SLE, and sarcoidosis are common non-infectious inflammatory diseases in FUO. VEXAS syndrome is characterized by recurrent inflammation and specific bone marrow findings.
keyPoints: Common NIIDs include adult-onset Still's disease, large-vessel vasculitis, polymyalgia rheumatica, SLE, and sarcoidosis.
VEXAS syndrome presents with recurrent inflammation, skin lesions, chondritis, lung disease, venous thrombosis, arthritis, myelodysplastic syndrome, and vacuoles in myeloid precursors.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Identifying Potentially Diagnostic Clues in FUO
items.content: The search for potentially diagnostic clues (PDCs) through thorough history-taking and physical examination is crucial in diagnosing Fever of Unknown Origin (FUO). PDCs, which include localizing signs, symptoms, and abnormalities, help narrow down probable diagnoses. However, they can be misleading, necessitating repeated evaluations.
keyPoints: PDCs are essential for creating a concise list of probable diagnoses.
History should cover fever pattern, medical history, drug use, travel, exposures, and animal contacts.
Physical examination must include eyes, lymph nodes, temporal arteries, liver, spleen, skin surface, and mucous membranes.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.title: Role of Initial Imaging in FUO
items.content: Despite their limitations, abdominal ultrasound and chest radiography are obligatory initial tests for all FUO patients. These low-cost, low-radiation tests help differentiate easily diagnosed cases from more complex ones.
keyPoints: Abdominal ultrasound and chest x-rays are preferred due to low cost and minimal side effects.
These tests help separate easily diagnosable diseases from others.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.title: Managing Medications in FUO Evaluation
items.content: Early discontinuation of all medications, including nonprescription drugs and supplements, is vital to exclude drug fever. If fever persists beyond 72 hours after stopping the suspected medication, it's unlikely to be the cause.
keyPoints: Discontinue medications early in evaluation for FUO.
Fever persisting beyond 72 hours post-discontinuation suggests non-drug-related fever.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.title: When and How to Use IGRA and TST for Tuberculosis Diagnosis
items.content: IGRA and TST are essential in evaluating tuberculosis, but their results can be indeterminate in certain conditions such as miliary tuberculosis or immunosuppression. A negative result does not exclude TB; thus, further diagnostic steps like liver biopsy may be necessary.
keyPoints: Indeterminate IGRA/TST results possible with miliary TB, malnutrition, immunosuppression, anti-IFN $ \gamma $ autoantibodies
Negative TST or IGRA does not exclude tuberculosis diagnosis
Liver biopsy for acid-fast smear, culture, PCR has highest diagnostic yield in suspected miliary TB
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 2)

items.title: Role of $ {}^{18} $F-FDG-PET/CT in Diagnosing Giant Cell Arteritis
items.content: $ {}^{18} $F-FDG-PET/CT is useful for diagnosing giant cell arteritis, especially when PDCs are misleading or absent. Temporal artery biopsy remains recommended for patients aged 55 and older.
keyPoints: High prevalence of giant cell arteritis in FUO, up to 17% among elderly
$ {}^{18} $F-FDG-PET/CT can diagnose large artery involvement but may miss temporal artery vasculitis due to vessel size and brain FDG uptake
Temporal artery biopsy recommended for patients ≥55 years
$ {}^{18} $F-FDG-PET/CT should be timed with elevated ESR or CRP for better diagnostic yield
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 2)

items.title: Advantages and Diagnostic Yield of $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT in FUO
items.content: $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT is a valuable imaging tool for diagnosing Fever of Unknown Origin (FUO) due to its high resolution, sensitivity in chronic low-grade infections, and accuracy in detecting central skeleton abnormalities. It outperforms traditional scintigraphy methods like gallium and leukocyte scintigraphy.
keyPoints: $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT has a diagnostic yield of ~50% for identifying the cause of FUO.
It is more effective than gallium scintigraphy and leukocyte scintigraphy in diagnosing FUO.
High resolution, greater sensitivity in chronic low-grade infections, and accuracy in central skeleton imaging are key advantages.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · $ ^{18} $F-FLUORDEOXYGLUCOSE POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY

items.title: Timing and Biomarker Considerations for $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT in FUO
items.content: The timing of $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT is crucial, especially during active inflammation. The diagnostic value increases when performed during a fever episode. Normal CRP and ESR levels at the time of scanning may limit its contributory outcome.
keyPoints: Correct timing during active inflammation enhances diagnostic value.
Normal CRP and ESR at scan time may reduce PET/CT's contributory outcome.
Periodic fever in FUO patients necessitates careful timing of the scan.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · $ ^{18} $F-FLUORDEOXYGLUCOSE POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY

items.title: Impact of Glucocorticoids on $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT Results in FUO
items.content: Glucocorticoid treatment can quickly reduce pathologic FDG uptake, which may obscure the PET/CT results. Therefore, glucocorticoids should be discontinued or postponed until after the scan to avoid masking underlying conditions.
keyPoints: Pathologic FDG uptake is rapidly reduced by glucocorticoids in diseases like vasculitis and lymphoma.
Glucocorticoid use should be stopped or postponed before $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT to prevent obscuring results.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · $ ^{18} $F-FLUORDEOXYGLUCOSE POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY

items.subtopic: ALTERNATIVES TO POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY/COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY | LATER-STAGE DIAGNOSTIC TESTS | SECOND OPINION IN AN EXPERT CENTER | Fever of Unknown Origin
items.title: Alternatives to PET/CT for FUO Diagnosis
items.content: When PET/CT is unavailable, consider alternative whole-body imaging modalities such as scintigraphy. Scintigraphy can localize active inflammation but has a lower diagnostic yield compared to PET/CT.
keyPoints: Gallium scintigraphy diagnostic yield: 21% to 54%
Leukocyte scintigraphy diagnostic yield: 8% to 31%
Full-body MRI may have a comparable diagnostic yield to PET/CT, but further studies are needed.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ALTERNATIVES TO POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY/COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY | LATER-STAGE DIAGNOSTIC TESTS | SECOND OPINION IN AN EXPERT CENTER | Fever of Unknown Origin

items.subtopic: ALTERNATIVES TO POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY/COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY | LATER-STAGE DIAGNOSTIC TESTS | SECOND OPINION IN AN EXPERT CENTER | Fever of Unknown Origin
items.title: Role of Second Opinion in FUO Management
items.content: Seeking a second opinion from an expert center can significantly improve diagnostic yield for patients with unexplained FUO. Expert centers often diagnose over half of such cases due to pattern recognition and high exposure.
keyPoints: Over 50% diagnosis rate at FUO expert centers
1 in 10 patients without a diagnosis after second opinion became fever-free upon empirical treatment
Beneficial outcome in under 70% of patients with unexplained FUO
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ALTERNATIVES TO POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY/COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY | LATER-STAGE DIAGNOSTIC TESTS | SECOND OPINION IN AN EXPERT CENTER | Fever of Unknown Origin

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a criterion for defining fever of unknown origin (FUO)?
options: Fever ≥38.3°C on at least two occasions
Illness duration of at least 4 weeks
items.explanation: The illness duration criterion for FUO is at least 3 weeks, not 4.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · Fever of Unknown Origin | DEFINITION

items.question: Which test is NOT obligatory in the evaluation of fever of unknown origin (FUO)?
items.explanation: MRI is not listed as an obligatory investigation in the evaluation of FUO.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · Fever of Unknown Origin | DEFINITION

items.question: What distinguishes inflammation of unknown origin (IUO) from fever of unknown origin (FUO)?
options: Presence of elevated inflammatory parameters without fever
items.explanation: IUO is defined by the presence of elevated inflammatory parameters on multiple occasions for at least 3 weeks in an immunocompetent patient with normal body temperature.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · Fever of Unknown Origin | DEFINITION

items.question: Which of the following factors has contributed to the decreased frequency of infective endocarditis as a cause of FUO?
options: Earlier detection by CT and ultrasound
items.explanation: Infective endocarditis is less frequent due to improved blood culture and echocardiographic techniques.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the most common cause of FUO in non-Western cohorts, according to the passage?
items.explanation: Infections, particularly Mycobacterium tuberculosis, remain the most common cause of FUO in non-Western cohorts.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is associated with a lower chance of finding a final explanation for FUO?
items.explanation: A normal PET/CT has been associated with a lower chance of finding a final diagnosis.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the 'FUO paradox' as described in the passage?
options: The proportion of undiagnosed patients has decreased despite technical advances.
Patients with FUO are diagnosed more quickly due to earlier medical consultation and advanced diagnostics.
Most patients with FUO have a final diagnosis within 3 weeks.
Infections are now the most common cause of FUO in Western cohorts.
items.explanation: 'The FUO paradox' refers to the higher proportion of undiagnosed patients despite technical advances, as only difficult cases continue to meet FUO criteria.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following infectious diseases is considered rare but important to consider in patients with FUO due to nonspecific symptoms?
items.explanation: Q fever (Coxiella burnetii) is rare but should be considered due to nonspecific symptoms.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In patients with FUO and unexplained central nervous system, gastrointestinal tract, or joint symptoms, which test should be performed early in the diagnostic process?
options: Polymerase chain reaction testing for T. whipplei
items.explanation: PCR testing for T. whipplei should be performed on stool and blood early in the diagnostic process.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition is characterized by sterile thrombotic disease occurring as a paraneoplastic phenomenon, especially with adenocarcinomas?
items.explanation: Marantic endocarditis is a sterile thrombotic disease occurring as a paraneoplastic phenomenon.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT commonly associated with drug-induced fever in patients with FUO?
items.explanation: Aspirin is not commonly associated with drug-induced fever in FUO.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the most important step in the diagnostic workup for patients presenting with FUO?
options: Searching for potentially diagnostic clues through history-taking, physical examination, and obligatory investigations
items.explanation: The most important step is the search for potentially diagnostic clues (PDCs) through complete and repeated history-taking and physical examination.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.question: Why should antibiotic and glucocorticoid treatment be stopped before further diagnostic tests are initiated in patients with FUO?
options: They can mask many diseases, making it difficult to obtain accurate test results
They increase the risk of developing new infections
They cause permanent changes in laboratory test outcomes
They interfere with imaging studies like ultrasounds and chest x-rays
items.explanation: Antibiotic and glucocorticoid treatment can mask many diseases, affecting the reliability of diagnostic tests.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.question: Which diagnostic test is considered valuable for screening in patients with FUO due to its low cost and absence of specific symptoms?
items.explanation: Investigation of cryoglobulins is considered a valuable screening test in patients with FUO due to its low cost and the absence of specific symptoms.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.question: When should further diagnostic tests be performed in patients with recurrent inflammation?
options: Before any history-taking and physical examination
items.explanation: Further diagnostic tests should be performed only during an inflammatory episode because abnormalities may be absent between episodes.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.question: In which condition may a tuberculin skin test (TST) or interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) yield indeterminate results?
items.explanation: Indeterminate results may occur in miliary tuberculosis, malnutrition, immunosuppression, or with anti-IFN-gamma autoantibodies.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 2)

items.question: Which diagnostic test is recommended for patients ≥55 years to diagnose giant cell arteritis?
items.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy is recommended for patients ≥55 years to diagnose giant cell arteritis.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 2)

items.question: What is the diagnostic yield of echocardiography in the absence of PDCs?
items.explanation: The diagnostic yield of echocardiography is very low in the absence of PDCs.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a reason why FDG uptake may obscure pathologic foci in certain organs?
options: FDG accumulates only in malignant cells, which can be present in these organs.
Physiologic uptake or concentration of FDG occurs in the brain, heart, bowel, kidneys, and bladder.
FDG is not taken up by tissues with a high rate of glycolysis.
Vascular uptake of FDG is decreased in patients with vasculitis.
items.explanation: Physiologic uptake or concentration of FDG may obscure pathologic foci in the brain, heart, bowel, kidneys, and bladder.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · $ ^{18} $F-FLUORDEOXYGLUCOSE POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY

items.question: What dietary measure can help prevent FDG uptake in the heart that may obscure endocarditis?
items.explanation: FDG uptake in the heart, which obscures endocarditis, may be prevented by consumption of a low-carbohydrate diet before the PET investigation.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · $ ^{18} $F-FLUORDEOXYGLUCOSE POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY

items.question: What is the approximate diagnostic yield of PET/CT in finding the final explanation for inflammation in FUO patients?
items.explanation: Meta-analyses report a high diagnostic yield of PET/CT in the workup of FUO patients, with total diagnostic yield of ~50% for PET/CT.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · $ ^{18} $F-FLUORDEOXYGLUCOSE POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY

items.question: Why should glucocorticoid use be stopped or postponed before performing $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT?
options: Glucocorticoids increase FDG uptake in malignant cells.
Pathologic FDG uptake is quickly eradicated by treatment with glucocorticoids.
Glucocorticoids reduce the resolution of PET/CT images.
Glucocorticoids cause false-positive results in leukocyte scintigraphy.
items.explanation: Pathologic FDG uptake is quickly eradicated by treatment with glucocorticoids in many diseases, including vasculitis and lymphoma; therefore, glucocorticoid use should be stopped or postponed until after $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT is performed.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · $ ^{18} $F-FLUORDEOXYGLUCOSE POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY

items.statement: The definition of FUO includes patients with known immunocompromised states.
items.explanation: FUO excludes patients with a known immunocompromised state.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · Fever of Unknown Origin | DEFINITION

items.statement: The causes for inflammation of unknown origin (IUO) and fever of unknown origin (FUO) are the same.
items.explanation: It has been shown that the causes for IUO and FUO are the same, hence their workup should be identical.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · Fever of Unknown Origin | DEFINITION

items.statement: In Western cohorts, infections are the most common cause of FUO.
items.explanation: Noninfectious inflammatory diseases (NIIDs) are the most common cause of FUO in Western cohorts.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The chance of remaining without a final diagnosis is higher in patients from Europe compared to those from Asia.
items.explanation: The passage states that the chance of remaining without a final diagnosis is two to five times higher in European patients.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: VEXAS syndrome is characterized by recurrent inflammation and skin lesions.
items.explanation: VEXAS syndrome includes symptoms like recurrent inflammation and skin lesions.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Factitious fever can be identified by simultaneous measurements at different body sites showing consistent temperature.
items.explanation: Factitious fever is identified by inconsistent temperatures across different body sites.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Abdominal ultrasound and conventional chest radiography are preferred over thoracic and abdominal CT as obligatory tests due to their higher sensitivity.
items.explanation: They are preferred due to lower cost, lower radiation burden, and absence of side effects, not higher sensitivity.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.statement: Multiple blood samples should be cultured long enough to ensure detection of fastidious organisms in patients with FUO.
items.explanation: It is critical to culture multiple blood samples long enough for the detection of fastidious organisms.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.statement: Miliary tuberculosis can be diagnosed with high sensitivity using TST or IGRA.
items.explanation: Both TST and IGRA have similar sensitivities, and a negative result does not exclude miliary tuberculosis.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 2)

items.statement: $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT is useful for diagnosing giant cell arteritis in patients with FUO.
items.explanation: $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT can be used to diagnose giant cell arteritis, especially when combined with elevated ESR or CRP.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 2)

items.statement: $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT directly provides a definitive diagnosis in all patients with FUO.
items.explanation: PET/CT and other scintigraphic techniques do not directly provide a definitive diagnosis, except in some cases like large vessel vasculitis.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · $ ^{18} $F-FLUORDEOXYGLUCOSE POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY

items.statement: FDG uptake mechanisms allow differentiation among infection, sterile inflammation, and malignancy.
items.explanation: The mechanisms responsible for FDG uptake do not allow differentiation among infection, sterile inflammation, and malignancy.

items.explanation: Empirical therapeutic trials are advised against except when the patient's condition worsens.

items.assertion: The term FUO refers to both fever of unknown origin and inflammation of unknown origin.
items.reason: FUO and IUO have the same causes and workup, making it convenient to use the term FUO for both within this chapter.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the term FUO is used for both conditions.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · Fever of Unknown Origin | DEFINITION

items.assertion: Infections are less common causes of FUO in Western Europe and the United States.
items.reason: Mycobacterium tuberculosis is a major cause of infections in non-Western nations but not as common in Western regions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with Mycobacterium tuberculosis being less common in Western Europe and the US.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: The FUO paradox is explained by an increase in diagnostic techniques.
items.reason: Patients with fever often seek medical advice earlier, leading to quicker diagnoses for most cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the paradox is due to difficult cases continuing to meet FUO criteria despite advances.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Travel to tropical countries should prompt consideration of endemic infectious diseases in FUO.
items.reason: Endemic diseases like malaria and leishmaniasis are more likely in these regions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Schnitzler's syndrome can be diagnosed by clinical criteria alone.
items.reason: It presents with urticaria, bone pain, and monoclonal gammopathy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: In patients with FUO, further diagnostic procedures should be limited to specific investigations aimed at confirming or excluding diseases on a concise list.
items.reason: Most investigations are helpful only for patients who have PDCs for the diagnoses sought.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.assertion: Immunologic serologies often yield false-positive results in patients with FUO.
items.reason: These tests are highly specific and useful without PDCs pointing to specific disorders.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because these tests more often yield false-positive rather than true-positive results and are of little use without PDCs.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · A Structured Diagnostic Approach (part 1)

items.assertion: FDG-PET/CT is more likely than alternative tests to correctly identify the cause of FUO.
items.reason: Indirect comparisons of diagnostic yields suggested that $^{18}$F-FDG-PET/CT was more effective in identifying the cause of FUO compared to other tests.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Fever of Unknown Origin · $ ^{18} $F-FLUORDEOXYGLUCOSE POSITRON EMISSION TOMOGRAPHY

items.assertion: Repeating PET/CT after an initial scan that did not contribute to diagnosis is usually of little value.
items.reason: New signs or symptoms must appear for a repeat scan to be potentially valuable.

items.reason: FUO is often an atypical presentation of common diseases, making pattern recognition by experts crucial.
